solutions.solution:
(vi) A ≥ B, B ≥ C
(ix) A ≥ C, B, B ≥ C
Sol:
Step I: We see that (i) and (ii) have four terms each and hence A > B is common term. Therefore, a combination of (i) and (ii) is not possible as they fall in step I itself. For all others, the terms in the given inequalities are three terms each and hence it is possible to combine them as they fall in step I itself. So, we apply step II and check whether (iii) is better than one term A, greater than another one C.
Step II: (iii) B less than one term A, greater than another one C. Combined inequality: A > B > C or C < B < A possible.
(iv) B less than (or equal to) C, and B is less than A, combination possible.
(v) B greater than (or equal to) both A and C, combination possible.
(vi) B less than one term C, greater than another term A, combination not possible.
Combined inequality: A < B < C or C > B > A
(vii) B less than (or equal to) both terms, combination not possible.
(viii) B greater than (or equal to) one term A and less than (or equal to) other term C, combination possible.
Combined inequality: C ≥ B ≥ A or A ≤ B ≤ C
(ix) B less than (or equal to) one term A and greater than (or equal to) other term C, combination possible.
Combined inequality: A ≥ B ≥ C or C ≤ B ≤ A
(x) B less than one term C and greater than (or equal to) other term, combination possible.
Combined inequality: A ≤ B < C or C > B ≥ A.
Step III: (iv), (vii) were rejected in step II.
Now, let us apply third golden rule to the combined inequality obtained in case of (iii), (v), (viii), (ix) and (x).
(iii) Combined inequality: A > B > C
Conclusion: A > C
(Because '>' sign does not appear twice in the combined inequality, it does not appear in the conclusion.)
(v) Combined inequality: A < B < C
Conclusion: A < C
(Because '<' sign does not appear twice in the combined inequality, it does not appear in the conclusion.)
(viii) Combined inequality: C ≥ B ≥ A
Conclusion: C ≥ A

Combined inequalities:
(i) Combined inequality: A ≥ B ≥ C
(ii) Because '≥' sign appears twice in the combined inequality, it does appear in the conclusion.
Combined inequality: A ≥ B ≥ C
Conclusion: A ≥ C
(iii) Because '<' sign appears twice in the combined inequality, it does appear in the conclusion.
Combined inequality: A < B < C
Conclusion: A < C
(iv) Because '<' sign does not appear twice; it appears only once, it does not appear in the conclusion.
The, our solution is:
(i) no combination possible.
(ii) do... A ≥ C. Conclusion: A > C
(iii) no combination possible.
(iv) yes... A ≥ C. Conclusion: A ≥ C
(v) no combination possible.
(vi) yes... B < C. Conclusion: A < C
(vii) no combination possible.
(viii) yes... C ≥ B ≥ A. Conclusion: C ≥ A
(ix) yes... A ≥ B ≥ C. Conclusion: A ≥ C
(x) A < B < C. Conclusion: A < C

19.5: The Strategy for Solving
We have had enough theoretical background by now to tackle the problem in a quick, neat and efficient fashion. Without wasting much space, I straight away come to the various steps needed to solve the problem.
First Step: Neatly decode.
The question itself tells you which code stands for which arithmetical operation. For example, in simple problem 19.2 we have given the codes P, Q, R, S which stands for +, -, x, ÷ respectively or 'a' by '>' wherever you see them.
Let P = Q means P > Q. Therefore replace 'a' by '>' and 'b' by '<'. You should take one thing replace 'P' by '>', 'Q' by '<', 'R' by '≥' and 'S' by '≤' or whatever the code at a time and replace it by original mathematical symbol in all the five questions before going to the step II. And you should do it quickly.
Second step: Take one conclusion at a time and decide which statements are relevant for evaluating that.
Now, this needs some thinking. What do we mean by relevant statements? By a relevant statement we mean the statement that is not useless for deriving to a conclusion. If there is a conclusion, say, x > y then a statement like b > c is useless because it does not help us neither x nor y. Therefore, any analysis of this statement can't tell us anything about the conclusion: x > y. For any such statement the relevant statements are those that can be combined to prove or disprove that conclusion. How do we find the relevant statements?
